paragraphs.context: Definitions ................................................................................................................... 37 Table 28 RaP Comphance Table ................................................................................................................................. 41 SEMI E139--0613 SEMI 2006, 2013 semr 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Specification is to specify the cooperative interaction between the Factory Infonnation & Control System (FICS) and the equipment to manage the specifications of equipment processing (for instance, equipment recipes). 1.2 There are six primary elements that must be supported to achieve this purpose: On-cool & Off-cool Recipe Managemelll - Support the management of multi-part recipes within the equipment as well as at the FICS level without the requirement that these management applications be integrated. Recipe Integrity - Guarantee that the recipes that define process execution on a piece of equipment are the same as those intended for that use within the FICS. Process Imegricy- Ensure that all configuration values/settings of the tool that can be changed by the user and which affect the process outcome can be managed by the host within the context of a process job. Adjustable Parameter Definition - Support the definition of recipe parameters to be used by other SEMI tandards (e.g., EMI E40) to adjust the parameter values in a consistent manner across all equipment. On-tool & Off-cool Recipe Crearion and Editing - Define a consistent interaction protocol between the FICS and the systems(s) that support recipe creation/editing functionality. Information Accessibility- Make key information about the recipes visible to the FLC 1.2. I See 7 for a discussion of how the purpose is achieved. A summary 1s contained in 7. I 3.

paragraphs.context: the definition from the OMG UML specification has precedence. 5.2 Abbreviations and Acronyms 5.2.1 FJCS - Factory lnfomiation and Control System 5.2.2 PDE - Process Definition Element 5.2.3 RaP- Recipe and Paran1eter Management (this specification) 5.2.4 UML - Unified Modeling Language 5.3 Definitions 5.3.1 Checksum - a single unique value calculated from a sequence of data (a file, a string, etc.) that uniquely identifies that data. It is sometimes called a "digital fingerprint" or a "message digest". No two (different) sequences of data are likely to have the same checksum. Therefore, a checksum can be used to check data integrity. A typical method is to ( I ) calculate the checksum for a data sequence; (2) deliver that data sequence and its checksum value to a separate entity; (3) the receiving entity recalculates the checksum value and compares it with the original from the sender. If the checksums are the same, the receiver is assured that the data has not been modified or corrupted. 5.3.2 Factory Infomwrion and Comrol System (FICS) - the software system that controls the operation of the factory and its equipment It may include such components commonly referred to as the ME , Station Controllers, Recipe Managers, etc. 5.3.3 Module Parameter - settings that affect processing on an equipment moduJe (typically a Process Module). For a Process Module, the Module Parameters might represent temperatures, pressures, flow rates, etc. An equipment recipe typically manipulates Module Parameters to accomplish its purpose. 5.3.4 Process Definition Elemellt (PDE) - an

paragraphs.context: Managers, etc. 5.3.3 Module Parameter - settings that affect processing on an equipment moduJe (typically a Process Module). For a Process Module, the Module Parameters might represent temperatures, pressures, flow rates, etc. An equipment recipe typically manipulates Module Parameters to accomplish its purpose. 5.3.4 Process Definition Elemellt (PDE) - an executable specification of an activity or process on an equipment. The recipe for a particular equipment activity may consist of multiple PDEs . A PDE is the smallest process definition unit that can be individuaJly managed with FICS participation or knowledge. Each PDE includes a PDEheader and a PDEbody. 5.3.5 PDEbody - the executable portion of a Process Definition Element. The PDEbody is typically contained with.in the PDE construct. In some cases, a PDEbody may exist as a separate entity, but it is always exclusively related to its PDE. 5.3.6 PDEeditor - a software system that provides the ability to create, delete, and modify a PDE. A PDEeditor may exist as a subsystem of an equipment or as a standalone system provided either by the manufacturer of the equipment or a third party. 5.3.7 PDEheader - the descriptive portion of a Process Definition Element. The PDEheader is a collection of information related to a PDEbody. This information is sufficient to manage and utilize the PDE without accessing its PDEbody. 5.3.8 uuid - universally unique identifier. This 128 bit field (often represented as a 36 character string) is calculated according to the standard ISO/I EC 1 1578: 1996 - Remote Procedure

This overview is intended to provide a general understanding of the RaP concepts so that the detailed requirements that follow can be more easily understood. 7.3 RaP Parricipam.s 7.4 RaP assumes that there are three participants in the management of recipes-the FIC , the Equipment, and the PDEeditor. When these provide RaP services, they become "RaPnodes"-the FICS becomes a FICSnode, the Equipment becomes an EquipmemNode, and the PDEeditor becomes an EditorNode. 7.5 These three participants must work together to achieve the goals of recipe management. They may exist as independent entities using defined message services (RaPnodes) or may be combined in some cases (as the editor is often integrated into the equipment). ince integrated modules need no standardized communications, RaP defines the services and data needed for these participants to exist independently as shown in Figure 2. 7.6 RaP Environment 7.6.1 In discussing recipe management, one can divide the topic into three log1caJly separate activities: Execution of recipes Creation and editing of recipes Tracking and storage of recipes 7.6.2 RaP does not specify exactly how to perform any of these three activities. However, these activities define the context in which RaP defined services and concepts are to be applied. The PDE construct, including the PDEheader, acts as a wrapper. 7. 1 2.1.6.2 There are two areas where RaP requirements may result in changes to an existing recipe structure. The first area is the requirement that an EquipmencNode be able to match a PDEheader with its external PDEbody. This would be done best by inserting the 11id value into the PDEbody. However, there is a "bodyChecksum" value in the PDEheader that can be used without changing the PDEbody. This would require recalculating the checksum of the PDEbody, however. The second area is the requirement that the user be allowed to specify which PDEparameters exist. The PDEbody must contain information about the user-specified PDEparameters. However, in the case of an existing recipe with no user-specified PDEparamerers, no change is necessary until the user edits the PDE to add such parameters. When the collection changes (i.e., a PDE is added or removed), then the EquipmentNode shall notify the FICS via the event data collection mechanism available on that equipment. Two events shall be defined to support this capability: PDEadded and PDEremoved. A data collection parameter, PDE/ist, shall be provided to contain list of the uids of the affected PDEs. In order to report changes to the collection efficiently, the equipment should make a reasonable attempt to combine mto a single report any changes that occur in close succession (e.g., PDEs in a single TransferC0111ainer or from a single de/e1ePDE request). 8.5.4.7.1 The Equipmen1Node shall define a Status Variable named "RecipeStoreChangeTime" that contains the time and date of the last change of the recipe collection on that equipment. The business practice of the FICS is that the latest version of each PDE available on the equipment should always be used. For that reason, PDEs are referenced by gid and the equipment always resolves these gids. The FIC records which PDEs were last used with each Master PDE. It detects when the current set differs from the previous execution of this Master PDE and seeks confirmation from the user that the change 1s appropriate. All PDEs used the last time that this Master PDE was executed are on the equipment. The user has updated one PDE that must now be used in place of an older version. paragraphs.context: Support for SEMI E30 works differently. In this case, there is no concept of a process job. Instead, it is a two-step process: (I) the recipe is "selected" for execution., and then (2) a ST ART command is given to begin execution. Both of these requests are made using the S2,F49 message (Enhanced Remote Command - see SEMI ES). The Remote Command message accepts parameter values ( called CEPV AL 's). Each CEPV AL has a corresponding identifier called CPNAME. R2-3.5 In the first step, the RCMD (remote command) is RCP-SELECT. The identifier of the recipe is contained in a CEPV AL. The 11id (or gid) of the Master PDE is supplied as the CEPV AL to identify the recipe. The supplier determines the CPNAME of this parameter. R2-3.6 In the second step, the S2,F49 message requests the ST ART remote command. In this message, the CEPV AL's contain parameters that apply to the activity. The equipment may have some pre-defined parameters. The PDEparameter values may also be specified here as CEPV AL 's. The name of the PDEparameter should be specified as the CPNAME. R2-7 Recipe Security R2-7.1 The use of the umversally unique identifier and checksum provide significant protection against misprocessing due to the use of the wrong recipe. However it is not completely secure from intentional tampering. R2-7.2 It is possible for someone to modify an existing PDE without changing the uid. To complete the deception, a new checksum would need to be calculated and included in the PDE. A PDE changed in this way might not be detected in some factory systems. R2-7.3 There is a way to address this problem and ensure that only the correct recipes are used. This assumes that there is a factory level recipe management system where the known-good PDEs are all logged and their authorization for use is recorded. This system should log the uid of each PDE, along with its checksum. R2-7.4 Recall that the equipment will check each PDE against its internal checksum value when the PDE is transferred to the equipment and most will check it again before execution. This ensures that each PDE is self consistent. R2-7.5 Whenever a recipe is to be used for

It is clear that some gerPDEdirecroryO requests may require the RaPi10de to return information from the header of all the recipes stored at this node. Reading bits of infomiation from hundreds of potentially large files can be time consuming. It is suggested that steps be taken to optimize this implementation. For instance, if key parts of the header from each PDE on the RaPnode are stored separately (e.g., in a database}, then retrieval of this information can be very fast. SEMI E139.0613 SEMI 2006, 2013 semr Buffer previously used PDEmaps - Where local recipe execution is expected to be used only in rare cases when the factory control system is not available, the equipment can help the user keep the factory in operation by buffering the PDEmaps used in recent remote processing operations. These PDEmaps would be keyed to the Master PDE and be offered to the user as a pick list with time last executed, the material processed, and other related information to help make the selection. Create and store PDEmaps - The most practical option for local multi-part recipe execution is to allow the user to create PDEmaps, as noted in the second bullet above, and store these PDEmaps locally. Each PDEmap would be given a name by the user and also be associated with the user that created it (user login). This last option is the most powerful option and should be provided with every RaP implementation. R5-2.4 There are variations on these basic choices. To do this: First develop a schema that defines extensions to one or more of the complex types defined in the standard schema, as shown below. <?xml version="l.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <xs:schema id="MapData" targetNamespace="urn:semi-org:xsd.El42-l.V0921.SubstrateMap_ACMEProductsinc" xmlns: sm="urn: semi-org: xsd. El42-l. V0921. SubstrateMap" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/200l/XMLSchema" xmlns="urn:semi-org:xsd.El42-l.V0921.SubstrateMap_ACMEProductsinc" elementFormDefault="qualified"> <xs:import namespace="urn:semi-org:xsd.El42-l.V0921.SubstrateMap" schemaLocation="El42-l-V0211-Schema.xsd"/> <xs:element name="MapData" type="sm:MapDataType"/> <xs:complexType name="SubstrateTypeExtension"> <xs:complexContent> <xs:extension base="sm:SubstrateType"> <xs:sequence> <xs:element ref="SupplierData" minOccurs="0"/> </xs:sequence> </xs:extension> </xs:complexContent> </xs:complexType> <xs:element name="SupplierData" type="SupplierDataType"/> <xs:complexType name="SupplierDataType"> <xs:sequence> <xs:element ref="SetupFile"/> <xs:element ref="TestSystem"/> <xs:element ref="TestProgram"/> <xs:element ref="Prober"/> <xs:element ref="Operator"/> <xs:element ref="ProbeCard"/> <xs:element ref="TestStartTime"/> <xs:element ref="TestEndTime"/> </xs:sequence> </xs:complexType> <xs:element name="SetupFile" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="TestSystem" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="TestProgram" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="Prober" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="Operator" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="ProbeCard" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="TestStartTime" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:element name="TestEndTime" type="SupplierValue"/> <xs:complexType name="SupplierValue"> <xs:attribute name="Value" type="xs:string"/> </xs:complexType> </xs:schema> SEMI E142.1-0225 SEMI 2005, 2025 semr R1-2 BinCodeMap Extension Example R 1-2.1 Second: Add the namespace of the extension to your instance file: R l-2.1.1 "xmlns:xsi=http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance". Now you can specify the attribute xsi:type in the Substrate element and include the extended data alongside the standard data. Both will be validated against their respective schemas. Refer to the example below that validates against the schema extension in R 1-1. <?xml version="l.0" encoding="utf-8" ?> <MapData xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/200l/XMLSchema-instance" xsi: schemaLocation="urn : semi-org : xsd.El42-l.V0921.SubstrateMap_ACMEProductslnc El42-l-V0921-Schema-Extension.xsd" xmlns:sme="urn:semi-org : xsd.El42-l.V0921.SubstrateMap_ACMEProductsinc"

paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E142.3-1016 (Reapproved 0225) SPECIFICATION FOR WEB SERVICES FOR SUBSTRATE MAPPING This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on December 10, 2024. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in February 2025; originally published July 2006; previously published February 2024. 1 Purpose l . l This Standard is meant to facilitate software vendors, service providers, and/or semiconductor manufactures to offer a cohesive, interoperable set of tools for the transfer of substrate maps among semiconductor entities. 1.2 This Specification maps the services of SEMI El42 to eXtensible Markup Language (XML) Web Services. The Web Services Definition Language (WSDL) representation of this Standard is contained in a separate document. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard defines how to transmit substrate maps defined in SEMI E 142, between semiconductor manufacturing sites. 2.2 This Standard defines authentication and non-repudiation of the inter-site connection. 2.3 This Specification provides the following: The XML Schema data types and elements used to support the transport of SEMI E 142. l defined <Map Data> elements. The WSDL message, portType and binding definitions used to support the operations necessary for the transport of SEMI E 142. l defined <Map Data> elements. 2.4 This Specification does not add new domain information or concepts to the SEMI E142 Specification. The only additions made are those needed to render useful WSDL or XML Schemas. The SEMI E142.1 schema file is imported without extensions or restrictions. OTICE:

These conventions include the interactions among SOAP nodes generating and accepting SOAP messages for the purpose of exchanging information along a SOAP message path. 5.2.5 web service - a Web service is a software system designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network. It has an interface described in a machine-processable format (specifically WSDL). Other systems interact with the Web service in a manner prescribed by its description using SOAP-messages, typically conveyed using HTTP with an XML serialization in conjunction with other Web-related standards. 6 Ordering Information 6.1 ot applicable. 7 Description 7.1 Services Mapping 7.1.1 Table I shows the web services-WSDL operations-and how they map to WSDL messages. The session is established via the Logon service and released by the Logo ff service. A Sessionld is generated by the service provider in response to the Logon service and returned to the client. The client must include this Sessionld for all service invocations within the session. When the client invokes the Logoffservice, the Sessionld becomes invalid and will no longer be accepted by the service provider. The service provider shall invoke the Logo ff service automatically if there is no session activity for a configurable period of time. 7.3 Session Management 7.3.1 The Sessionld should be globally unique for every individual session. The Sessionld is not part of the SOAP message body, but rather transmitted in a 'Ticket' member of the SOAP header, named SyncHeader. In

In this way the handling of the session can be transparent. 7.4 Non-Repudiation 7.4.1 The service provider will maintain secure audit trails that include, at a minimum, for every service invocation; usemame, date, time, Sessionld, service name and service parameters. The Sessionld in particular can be used to compare with audit trails on the client side to prove that a transaction took place. paragraphs.context: of a SOAP request message may occur at the protocol level and at the receiver's application level, once the various layers dealing with the transport of the SOAP request process the message and deliver it to the 'business' layers of the application. The management of non-application specific errors is not part of this Specification as they are already defined properly by their respective standards. Application level errors are managed in SEMI El42.3 through well-identified WS messages. The receiver will generate a response through predefined fault messages. If an error is expected to be frequent, therefore predictable, a specific fault message is created for this recurring error case. The best example is an expired Session Id, for instance. 7.6.2 Table S describes the SOAP Fault messages that have been identified for each Web Service. MapDownloadFault Generic fault structure. Matches all cases of unpredictable download failures. MapGroupDownload This service is not supported in this Specification. 7.3 Stream 21 Message Usage 7.3.1 Stream 21 Item Transfer messages were added to SEMl ES for transferring small and large substrate map data. When transferring large data that cannot be stored in a single SECS-II data item, the Stream 21 messages allow the data to be split into smaller item parts for transfer, where each part is transferred as a separate ITEMPART data item in one or more messages. It is assumed that the receiver of the item parts will concatenate the item parts to recreate the whole item. Refer to SEMl E5 for details. 7.3.2 Stream 21 Transfer Techniques 7.3.2.1 SEMI E5 defines two Stream 21 transfer techniques: Single Message technique (S21F5/F6 or S21FI/F2 followed by S21F3/F4) Multiple Message technique (S2 I Fl5/Fl6 or S2 I FI3/FI4 followed by one or more S2IFI 7/FI 8 messages).
